title: 📊 Your IRS Rights: What They Cannot Do To You
description:
What the IRS can and cannot legally do, with citations to the Taxpayer Bill of Rights and recent court cases. Plus the UK equivalent rights when dealing with HMRC.

Personal finance, taxpayer rights, IRS audit rights, HMRC rights 2026.

segments.voice: The IRS has more power than almost any other US government agency to garnish your wages, levy bank accounts, and seize property. But it does not have unlimited power. The Taxpayer Bill of Rights, codified in IRC section 7803(a)(3), gives you ten specific rights you can invoke whenever the IRS contacts you. Most people do not know they exist.
segments.visual: taxpayer bill of rights document IRS

segments.voice: Right one, the right to be informed. The IRS must explain in plain language why they are contacting you, what you owe, and how it was calculated. If you receive a notice that lacks clear explanation of the underlying issue, you can request clarification in writing. Notice CP14, CP90, CP504, each has specific meaning and the IRS website explains every code.

Right two, the right to quality service. You can demand a supervisor if an IRS employee is rude, unhelpful, or providing wrong information. Per IRM 1 dot 4 dot 51, every employee must identify themselves with name, badge number, and direct contact information. Document the call, write down the time and the badge number, and request a supervisor immediately when service is poor.

Right three, the right to pay no more than the correct amount of tax. You owe what the law requires, not what the IRS computer initially calculates. Many notices arise from automated under-reporter matches that miss legitimate income items, deductions, or credits. The Government Accountability Office has documented that roughly seventeen percent of CP2000 notices are incorrect on the first pass.

Right four, the right to challenge IRS positions and be heard. You can submit documentation, arguments, and evidence in response to any proposed IRS adjustment. Per IRC section 6213(b)(2), the agency must consider your response before assessing the additional tax. If they ignore your evidence, you have grounds to appeal.

Right five, the right to appeal in an independent forum. If you disagree with an audit result, you can request a Collection Due Process hearing or appeal to the IRS Office of Appeals. The Office of Appeals is structurally separate from the audit division per IRS Restructuring and Reform Act of nineteen ninety-eight. Appeals officers settle a high percentage of cases, often at fifty cents on the dollar or less.

Right six, the right to finality. The IRS has a ten-year statute of limitations to collect assessed tax under IRC section 6502, with limited extensions. The audit statute is generally three years from filing, six years if there is substantial under-reporting of twenty-five percent or more, and unlimited if you filed a fraudulent return or never filed at all.

Right seven, the right to privacy. The IRS cannot disclose your tax return information to third parties without your consent or court order, per IRC section 6103. Identity thieves often impersonate the IRS via phone calls and emails. The real IRS will never demand immediate payment by gift card, threaten arrest, or contact you first by email. All initial contact is by physical mail.

Right eight, the right to confidentiality. Communications with your CPA, enrolled agent, or attorney about tax matters are privileged in many circumstances per IRC section 7525. The privilege is narrower than attorney-client privilege but still protects most communications about tax compliance. Privilege can be lost if you forward those communications to others, so keep them tight.

Right nine, the right to retain representation. You can have an attorney, CPA, or enrolled agent represent you in any IRS matter. You sign Form 2848 Power of Attorney to authorize them. After that, the IRS must direct communications to your representative. You do not have to talk to the IRS yourself once you have representation in place.

Right ten, the right to a fair and just tax system. The Taxpayer Advocate Service, an independent organization within the IRS, exists to help when normal channels are not working. Call seven seven seven seven seven seven seven if you have an immediate hardship. The Taxpayer Advocate publishes an annual report that documents the worst IRS practices and pushes for reform.

What the IRS legally cannot do. They cannot show up at your house unannounced, generally. Most contact is mail-first. They cannot demand cash or gift cards as payment. They cannot threaten you with arrest by Border Patrol or local police, those are scam tactics. The Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration tracks these scams at tigta dot gov.

Levy and garnishment, the actual collection process. Before the IRS can levy your bank account or garnish wages, they must send a Final Notice of Intent to Levy, IRS Letter 1058 or LT11, giving you thirty days to respond. During that thirty-day window, you can request a Collection Due Process hearing under IRC section 6330, which freezes the levy.

If a levy hits anyway, banks must hold the funds for twenty-one days before sending them to the IRS. During those twenty-one days, you can call the IRS, request a release, or set up an installment agreement to stop the levy. Many people panic and pay everything immediately when they could have negotiated a manageable payment plan.

Offer in compromise, settling for less than full owed. If you genuinely cannot pay the full balance, the IRS Offer in Compromise program lets you settle for less. The form is 656 with a 433-A financial disclosure. Acceptance rates have run between thirty-eight and forty-two percent in recent years per IRS Data Book. The application fee is two hundred and five dollars unless you qualify for a low-income waiver.

Installment agreements, the more common path. If you owe under fifty thousand dollars, you can usually set up a streamlined installment agreement online at irs.gov over six years. Above that threshold, you submit financial information and the agreement gets reviewed manually. The setup fee ranges from thirty-one to two hundred and twenty-five dollars depending on payment method.

Currently not collectible status. If your income and assets are too low to pay anything reasonably, you can request CNC status under IRC section 6343(e). The IRS pauses collection but the debt does not disappear, interest keeps accruing. Once your circumstances improve, the IRS resumes collection. CNC is best as a temporary buffer, not a long-term solution.

Audit defense basics. If you receive an audit letter, you have thirty days to respond unless the letter says otherwise. Do not call the IRS first. Get organized, gather your records, and consider hiring an enrolled agent or CPA. The hourly rate for representation is typically two hundred to four hundred dollars and routinely saves multiples of that in adjustments.

What to bring to an audit. Receipts, bank statements, mileage logs, calendars, contracts, anything that supports the position on your return. The IRS audit guide for each industry, called the Market Segment Specialization Program, is publicly available on irs.gov and tells you exactly what auditors will ask for. Read the relevant guide for your business type before the meeting.

Penalty abatement, often missed. If you have a clean compliance history and a one-time mistake, you can request a first-time penalty abatement under IRM section 20 dot 1 dot 1. This is essentially automatic if you qualify, but the IRS does not volunteer it. You have to ask. Late filing and late payment penalties for one tax year are commonly waived under this rule.

Reasonable cause penalty relief. Beyond first-time abatement, you can request relief based on reasonable cause if you had a serious illness, natural disaster, or other significant disruption. The IRS reviews these case by case. Document everything, hospital records, insurance claims, FEMA disaster declarations, anything that shows the cause was real and outside your control.

UK readers, HMRC equivalent rights. The HMRC Charter, last updated in twenty twenty-three, gives similar rights, including the right to be treated fairly, helped to understand obligations, and the right to challenge through the Adjudicator and the First-Tier Tax Tribunal. The Tax Adjudicator handles complaints when normal HMRC channels fail to resolve issues fairly.

Practical mindset shift. The IRS is not your enemy, but they are not your friend either. They are an enforcement agency following procedures. The procedures favor the taxpayer who knows their rights and the agency staff are trained not to volunteer those rights. Knowledge is the difference between paying a hundred percent of a disputed amount and paying ten percent.
segments.visual: person reading tax law calmly informed

segments.voice: Subscribe to FinanzasClara and hit the bell. What's coming next week, the exact letter a Reddit user sent that got an IRS levy released in eight days, plus the supporting documents the IRS asked for. Drop a comment with your scariest IRS notice and I will work through the response process in a follow-up video.
